status.label: Proposed — application in progress
status.detail: An application has been submitted to the state. The board has been asked to resubmit and is preparing a sharper, corridor-focused resubmission. Nothing on this site should be read as a granted designation.

boundary.description: The Lake Forest Boulevard commercial corridor, from Crowder Boulevard to Bullard Avenue — roughly three walkable miles along New Orleans East's original main street.

hero.kicker: New Orleans East · Crowder Blvd → Bullard Ave
hero.title: Three miles of main street, ready for its second act.
hero.lede: The Lake Forest Cultural District is a proposed state designation that would make the older commercial buildings along Lake Forest Boulevard eligible for Louisiana's Commercial Historic Tax Credit — putting real capital behind owners who restore the corridor, and protecting the cultural identity that was always here.
hero.welcome: Open to everyone. Built for the people already here.

story.title: A district for all people.
story.body: A Louisiana Certified Cultural District is not a gate, a fence, or a brand for a select few. It is a designation that recognizes what a community already is — and unlocks a financial tool for the people who own, lease, and love its buildings. The Lake Forest Cultural District welcomes everyone: residents, business owners, families in the surrounding neighborhoods, and visitors from across the city and beyond.

story.kicker: Chapter Two — Where it came from
story.title: The East was master-planned as its own downtown.
story.body: In the 1960s and 70s, New Orleans East was marketed as "Orlandia — the newest New Orleans": a quarter of the city laid out with its own commercial heart. Lake Forest Boulevard was built to be that heart — a main-street, downtown-style hub serving a growing community. Those buildings are still standing. And by the historic tax credit program's own measure, they are now crossing into historic age.
story.note: The 50-year eligibility line is rolling, not fixed. Standing in 1970, a 1920 building had just become historic. Standing here today, the corridor built in the 1970s is crossing that exact same line.

story.title: A tax credit, not a handout.
story.body: Louisiana's State Commercial Historic Tax Credit returns 25% of qualified rehabilitation expenses (35% in rural areas) to owners — or long-term lessees responsible for improvements — who restore income-producing historic buildings. The credit is transferable, carries forward five years, sits under an $85M annual program cap, and sunsets December 31, 2028. The Cultural District designation is the eligibility gateway for corridor buildings that are not on the National Register and not in a Downtown Development District.

story.title: Culture that's already here.
story.body: The corridor is anchored by Joe W. Brown Memorial Park and the Louisiana Nature Center — green space, water, and community institutions that most commercial strips can only envy. The goal of the district is not to import a culture. It is to restore, protect, and expand the one the community already built.

story.title: Small, visible steps. Then momentum.
story.body: Trust is built by working together on things people can see. The plan is deliberately focused: one corridor, one message, the buildings that already qualify. Rehabilitate existing properties — no new construction proposed, no residential component, and no request for city funding. Bring developers to the table with a concrete offer, and let finished storefronts make the next argument.

facts.act: Louisiana Certified Cultural Districts were created by Act 298 of 2007.
facts.gateway: The Cultural District designation is the eligibility gateway for older commercial buildings on the Lake Forest corridor to access the Louisiana State Commercial Historic Tax Credit — the path for buildings not on the National Register and not in a Downtown Development District.
facts.creditSummary: 25% of qualified rehabilitation expenses (35% rural), transferable, five-year carryforward, $85M annual program cap, sunsets December 31, 2028.
facts.rolling50: Historic-age eligibility runs on a rolling 50-year window, not a fixed date. The common disqualifier is loss of historic integrity through remodeling — not age.
facts.artExemptionEliminated: The original-art local sales-tax exemption was eliminated effective January 1, 2025 (Act 11). It is not a current benefit and is not presented as one anywhere on this site.

is: An eligibility gateway to the State Commercial Historic Tax Credit for corridor buildings
A benefit that flows to property owners and qualifying long-term lessees who invest in their own buildings
A place-based revitalization tool created by Act 298 of 2007
A designation that recognizes and protects the corridor's existing cultural identity
Open and accessible to all people
isNot: Not a grant — no money is handed to any group, board, or person
Not a fund anyone can apply to
Not an art sales-tax exemption — that benefit was eliminated January 1, 2025 (Act 11)
Not a claim that any owner has committed to any project

workedExample.title: What the numbers look like on a real project
workedExample.note: Illustrative and rounded for planning purposes. The New Markets benefit is shown at an illustrative 20% midpoint and depends on securing an allocation. Not tax or legal advice.

rows.label: Total qualified rehabilitation work (owner funds all of it)
New Markets Tax Credit — outside investment, helps up front (~20%, illustrative)
Out-of-pocket project cost to the owner
State Commercial Tax Credit — 25% of qualified expenses, recovered after the work
Estimated net project cost after both credits

workedExample.transferNote: If the owner has no Louisiana tax bill to apply the credit against, it is fully transferable — it can be sold for cash, roughly $401,200 at 90¢ on the dollar.

eligibility.meaning: At least 50 years old — a rolling line buildings keep crossing every year.
Inside the certified Cultural District. This is the qualifying door for Lake Forest.
Still recognizable as historic — not stripped or remodeled beyond recognition.
Income-producing: commercial space, or residential rental.
Work follows the Secretary of the Interior's Standards for Rehabilitation.

footer.line: A community-led effort to restore the Lake Forest corridor through Louisiana's Cultural District program.
footer.disclaimer: This is a concept site for stakeholder review. Figures are drawn from program documents and parish assessor data and are subject to confirmation. Nothing here is tax or legal advice, and nothing implies a commitment by any property owner.
